main_summary.summary: Rev. Wheeler Parker Jr., first cousin of Emmett Till and the last living eyewitness to the August 1955 abduction, recounts his oral history with interviewer Joe Mosnier in Summit (Argo), Illinois, on May 23, 2011. Parker describes his childhood in Money, Mississippi, his 1947 family move to Argo, and the August 1955 visit to his grandfather Moses Wright's home during which he was present at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market when Till encountered Carolyn Bryant Donham. He recounts the late-night August 28 abduction at gunpoint by Roy Bryant and J.W. Milam, his prayer through the bedroom search, his return to Chicago via train through Memphis, and the funeral at Mamie Till-Mobley's home. Parker traces the case's long dormancy and revival, including Rich Samuels's 1985 NBC documentary, Keith Beauchamp's later film, the 2004 FBI reopening, Alvin Sykes's role in securing the Till Bill signed by George W. Bush, his meetings with Eric Holder at the US Justice Department, and the dedication of the inaugural Mississippi Freedom Trail marker at the Bryant's Grocery site.
key_themes: Religious conversion under threat of death
Federal reopening of cold-case civil rights murders
main_summary.historical_significance: Parker is the last surviving eyewitness to the events at Bryant's Grocery on August 24, 1955, and to the early-morning August 28 abduction from Moses Wright's home in Money, Mississippi. His testimony anchors the canonical chronology of the Till case from inside the Wright family household, distinguishing what he directly witnessed (Till's whistle, the rush to the car, the night-time abduction, the bedroom search by Bryant and Milam) from later embellishments by people who were not present. The interview captures Parker's later role as a public memory-keeper, his collaboration with Alvin Sykes on the Till Bill, his meetings with Eric Holder at the Justice Department during the 2004-2007 FBI reopening, and his participation in the inaugural Mississippi Freedom Trail marker ceremony. The recording is a primary source for the institutional memorialization of the Till case from 1985 forward.

chapters.title: Mississippi Freedom Trail Marker
chapters.summary: Parker opens with his recent visit to Mississippi for the inaugural Mississippi Freedom Trail marker ceremony at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market. He describes the event as the first of 25 planned markers, situates the site in relation to where he lived and went to school, and reflects on what the marker says about 1955 and the present.
related_events: Inaugural Mississippi Freedom Trail marker ceremony
Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market site dedication

chapters.title: Money Mississippi Childhood
chapters.summary: Parker describes growing up in Money, Mississippi, a town of about 50 people, with his grandfather Moses Wright and grandmother. He recounts cotton-picking, the one-room church school, the Delta soil, the sawmill next door, and the family move to Summit (Argo), Illinois, in 1947. He describes his grandfather as a no-nonsense man of dignity and his mother as a school teacher.

chapters.title: Behind the Iron Curtain
chapters.summary: Parker describes his awareness as a 16-year-old in 1955 that traveling to Mississippi was, as people said, like going behind the Iron Curtain. He had heard the stories of what could happen to Black people there and knew there was no legal protection. He contrasts this consciousness with the fun of swimming, jokes, and his same-age uncle driving the car.
related_events: August 1955 Wright family visit to Money, Mississippi

chapters.title: Bryant's Grocery Encounter
chapters.summary: Parker recounts the late afternoon at Bryant's Grocery and Meat Market when Emmett Till came in behind him, Simeon Wright followed, and Carolyn Bryant Donham came out of the store. Till whistled. Parker describes the immediate apprehension, the rush to the car, the dropped cigarette, and the high-speed escape down a gravel road with a car behind them throwing dust.

chapters.title: Saturday Shopping and Four Fifths
chapters.summary: Parker describes the days between Wednesday's incident and Sunday's abduction. Thursday and Friday passed without incident. On Saturday, August 27, the cousins traveled to Greenwood for shopping and stopped at a juke joint called Four Fifths. He recounts Emmett's playful nature, the wolf-whistle pattern not being the first time Till had drawn attention, and Till's sensitive reaction when their uncle Maurice Wright ran over a dog on the way home.

chapters.title: The Abduction at 2:30 AM
chapters.summary: Parker describes waking around 2:30 AM on Sunday, August 28, hearing the men say they were looking for a fat boy from Chicago. He describes praying through the bedroom search, the flashlight and pistol passing by him as he pretended to sleep, the search through Moses Wright's house, and the abductors finally taking Till from Simeon Wright's room. He stayed awake for 24 hours afterward.

chapters.title: Train Home and the Funeral
chapters.summary: Parker describes his uncle taking him to the train station, the confused stop in Memphis on the way to Chicago where he encountered segregated washroom signs, and his arrival at Mamie Till-Mobley's house on 64th and St. Lawrence. He describes the family in grief before the body was found, his trip back to Argo, and a later moment in town when he saw a juror who looked like one of Till's killers and felt his heart stop.

chapters.title: Religious Conversion and Conviction
chapters.summary: Parker describes the promise he made to God during the abduction night and how it took him six years, until age 22, to keep it. He explains that his religious upbringing taught him to handle the trauma by exhaling and letting it go. He describes his conviction since age 22 as a hard sell in his twenties but the source of his peace through five decades.
related_events: Wheeler Parker Jr. religious conversion circa 1961

chapters.title: Rich Samuels and the Case Revival
chapters.summary: Parker describes the long period of public silence about the Till case until 1985, when NBC's Rich Samuels did a documentary while covering the killing of basketball player Benjamin "Ben" Wilson Jr. in Chicago. Parker says before that, the prevailing attitude was that Till got what he deserved. He describes Reverend Martin Luther King's visit to Chicago in 1966 and the animosity he witnessed, and his Army service from 1962 to 1965.
related_events: 1985 Rich Samuels NBC documentary on Emmett Till

chapters.title: Keith Beauchamp Film and FBI Reopening
chapters.summary: Parker describes the events surrounding Keith Beauchamp's film and the 2004 FBI reopening of the Till case, led by the US Justice Department working with Mississippi authorities. He emphasizes Alvin Sykes's pivotal role in pursuing the case, the Till Bill signed by George W. Bush allocating $11 million per year for ten years toward civil rights cold cases, and Sykes's separate pursuit of the appropriations. He recounts his meeting with Eric Holder at the Justice Department.
related_events: 2004-2007 FBI reopening of Emmett Till case
Till Bill signing by George W. Bush
Justice Department meeting with Eric Holder

chapters.title: Closure and Mississippi Memorialization
chapters.summary: Parker explains he wants closure and to know what happened, not punishment. He describes the grand jury declining to indict Carolyn Bryant Donham, his University of Mississippi visit, and his perspective on the Tallahatchie County markers at the river site, the funeral home, the courthouse in Sumner, and the barn. He addresses Senator Jordan's role in presenting the Emmett Till Highway memorial to the body and the repeated vandalism of the sign.

chapters.title: Teaching the Next Generation
chapters.summary: Parker describes telling the story to school children and college students, finding younger audiences more receptive than older ones. He talks about white participation in the civil rights movement, citing Abraham Lincoln, Kennedy and Johnson signing civil rights legislation, the Freedom Riders, and Martin Luther King's commitment to nonviolence. He closes by saying the case revived after long dormancy and that young people will have to make the changes.

loc_video.caption: Wheeler Parker oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Chicago, Illinois, 2011 May 23
